_warning: HAND-AUTHORED. A sync script that writes this file is a bug. No upstream source has THEORIES as entities — the fandom's iceberg lives in forum threads and video essays, never as data with chapter stamps — so every row here is hand-typed. verified:false means Shawn has not confirmed the chapters against the manga yet.
_why_this_file_exists: The atlas charts what the reader KNOWS as of a chapter. A theory is the other half of reading One Piece: the thing the reader WONDERS. This file charts the fandom's iceberg the same way the map charts islands — every theory surfaces at a chapter, gathers evidence chapter by chapter, and one day flips to confirmed or debunked ON a chapter. Scrub the dial and watch the iceberg melt.
_semantics: surfaces_at_chapter is when the theory becomes chartable — the fruit_reveals rule, applied to a QUESTION: it must not surface before every noun in its title is on the page (a chapter-500 reader must never see the string 'Imu'), and never before its first evidence row. Each evidence row gates on its own chapter. status is resolved by status_timeline: the active status at chapter N is the last window whose from_chapter <= N — so a theory can be open for 400 chapters and flip to confirmed the chapter you watch it happen.
_tiers: 1 = surface (the questions every reader asks in East Blue). 2 = waterline. 3 = submerged (you had to be paying attention). 4 = deep (the fandom's long arguments). 5 = abyss (the questions the whole story is steering toward).
_statuses: open = the story has not answered. partly_confirmed = the story answered a piece of it on the page. confirmed = answered on the page. debunked = the story said no. SBS answers count as partly_confirmed at best and say so in the source_ref — the page outranks the postscript.
_before_any_content_ships: Every row is verified:false. Shawn must confirm every chapter against the manga and flip verified to true. Until then the UI renders these as unverified.

theories.title: What does the D. stand for?
theories.question: A single letter rides in the middle of the names the World Government fears most. Somebody knows what it means, and it is not the reader.

evidence.claim: Kureha, watching fireworks over Drum: 'the Will of D. lives on' — the first time anyone says the letter OUT LOUD as a thing.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Dr. Kureha invokes the Will of D. as Chopper sails away (~ch. 154). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Whitebeard to Blackbeard at Marineford: Teach is not 'the man Roger is waiting for' — the D. carries an inheritance, and it is selective.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Whitebeard's dying speech names an inheritor and denies Teach the title (~ch. 576). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Roger asks Whitebeard, of all things, about the meaning of the D. — the man who reached the end wanted to know what his own name meant.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Oden flashback: Roger and Whitebeard's night of drinking and questions (~ch. 966). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The queen who defied the World Government eight hundred years ago signed her letter 'Nefertari D. Lili'. The letter is older than the fear of it.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Cobra's audience: Lili's full name revealed (~ch. 1085). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Named on the page, never explained on it.

theories.title: Why is the last island called what it is called?
theories.question: The island at the end of the Grand Line has a name sailors repeat like a rumor. The spelling turned out to be the first clue.

evidence.claim: Crocus, at the twin capes, names the final island — the place only Roger's crew ever reached.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Crocus tells the crew of the final island at Reverse Mountain's exit (~ch. 105). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Roger, standing on it, laughs and names it LAUGH TALE — 'what a funny story'. The name was never a place-name; it was a review.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Roger flashback: the naming of Laugh Tale (~ch. 967). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A rumor with a name.
Roger named it laughing. WHAT he laughed at is a different theory, further down the iceberg.

theories.title: The All Blue exists
theories.question: A sea where the fish of all four seas swim together. Every cook who mentions it gets laughed at, and one of them staked his life on it anyway.

evidence.claim: Sanji names his dream: the All Blue, the legendary sea every chef dismisses as a fairy tale.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Sanji's dream established at the Baratie (~ch. 56). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Still a cook's fairy tale. The Grand Line keeps hinting the seas were once one sea.

theories.title: Luffy's devil fruit was never what the book said
theories.question: A back-of-the-book paramecia that the World Government spent eight hundred years chasing. The name in the encyclopedia was the cover story.

evidence.claim: Who's-Who — a former CP9 agent — was jailed for losing custody of, of all things, the Gomu Gomu no Mi. Nobody guards a rubber fruit that hard.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Who's-Who tells Jinbe why he left CP9 (~ch. 1017). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The Five Elders discuss a fruit that has not awakened in centuries — one the government renamed to bury it.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Gorosei conversation over the Wano battle (~ch. 1037). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The book was wrong on purpose: Hito Hito no Mi, Model Nika — a mythical zoan, awakened. The rubber was the warm-up act.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The fruit's true name stated during Luffy's awakening (~ch. 1044). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Why would CP9 guard rubber?
Renamed and reclassified on the page. The encyclopedia lied for eight centuries.

theories.title: An ancient weapon is alive and shy
theories.question: One of the three weapons that could end the world is not a machine at all.

evidence.claim: Neptune says it plainly: his daughter is Poseidon — the ancient weapon is a mermaid who is afraid of everything.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Neptune reveals Shirahoshi's identity during the Fish-Man Island arc (~ch. 626). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The power shown on the page: the Sea Kings answer her, and remember a promise made centuries ago.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Shirahoshi commands the Sea Kings; the ancient promise is recalled (~ch. 649). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Stated by a king, demonstrated by the sea.

theories.title: The swordsman's blood comes from Wano
theories.question: A boy from an East Blue village fights with a style a closed country would recognize, and old men in that country keep staring at his face.

evidence.claim: Zoro's black blade Shusui belongs to Wano's hero Ryuma — and Wano treats Zoro's swordsmanship as something coming home.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Shusui/Ryuma thread through Zoro's Wano chapters (~ch. 953). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Kawamatsu and Hyogoro, mid-battle: he is the image of Shimotsuki Ushimaru, the last daimyo of Ringo.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The resemblance remarked on the page (~ch. 1023). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A face old men keep recognizing.
The lineage is drawn on the page but never said to Zoro; an SBS traces the family tree. The postscript is not the page — see source_ref.

theories.title: The revolutionary murdered the king
theories.question: The world's newspapers convicted a revolutionary of regicide. The reader was not shown the room — which is always the tell.

evidence.claim: The headline: Sabo killed King Cobra at the Reverie. The world believes it instantly.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The news of Cobra's death and Sabo's blame breaks after Wano (~ch. 1054). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The room, finally shown: the king asked one question too many, and it was not Sabo who answered it. The story pinned its own crime on a witness.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Reverie flashback shows what actually killed Cobra (~ch. 1085). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A headline without a witness.
The flashback shows the real killer. The frame-up was the point.

theories.title: Who was Joy Boy?
theories.question: A name from the Void Century that keeps apologizing. He broke a promise eight hundred years ago, and the sea has been waiting for him to come back and keep it.

evidence.claim: Fish-Man Island keeps an apology addressed to it from a man called Joy Boy — a letter carved in stone, in the forbidden language.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Joy Boy's apology poneglyph enters the story at Fish-Man Island (~ch. 628). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Roger's crew reaches the last island, learns who Joy Boy was — and laughs. The reader is left outside the panel.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Laugh Tale flashback; the crew learns Joy Boy's story off-page (~ch. 967). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Zunesha, who has walked the sea for eight hundred years, hears the drums and says it: 'Joy Boy has returned.'
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Zunesha's declaration as Luffy's awakening begins (~ch. 1043). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Vegapunk's broadcast gives him a title at last: Joy Boy was the first pirate — a man who sailed before the word existed.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Vegapunk's posthumous message names Joy Boy the first pirate (~ch. 1114). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A name that apologizes.
The first pirate — title confirmed, man still unnamed. The iceberg keeps most of this one.

theories.title: What was Zunesha's crime?
theories.question: An elephant the size of an island was sentenced to walk the sea forever. The sentence is on the page; the crime never is.

evidence.claim: Zunesha speaks: it committed a crime long ago and may only act when ordered — the walking IS the sentence.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Zunesha's voice reaches Momonosuke during the Zou attack (~ch. 821). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Zunesha recognizes the drums of liberation the instant they sound — whatever the crime was, it happened close enough to Joy Boy to know his heartbeat.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Zunesha names Joy Boy's return unprompted (~ch. 1043). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The sentence without the charge. The leading read: the crime was helping.

theories.title: The lost warship sleeps under the closed country
theories.question: Crocodile tore Alabasta apart for a weapon that was never there. The stone he wanted read was a map to somewhere else — a country that sealed itself shut.

evidence.claim: The name PLUTON enters the story: a warship from the ancient past, worth a kingdom to a warlord.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Crocodile's true objective in Alabasta (~ch. 218). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Robin asks about Pluton in Wano, and nobody laughs — the question lands like a fact.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Robin raises Pluton with Sukiyaki after the raid (~ch. 1053). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Sukiyaki confirms it: Pluton lies beneath Wano, and opening the country's borders is what would let it out.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The former shogun explains what Wano's walls are actually holding (~ch. 1055). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The question that lands like a fact.
Stated by the man whose family sealed it in.

theories.title: Blackbeard's body is not built like other people's
theories.question: One man, two devil fruits — a thing the world's own rules say should kill him. The people who knew him longest keep saying the same strange word: different.

evidence.claim: Shanks carries three scars he did not get from an enemy of legend — he got them from Teach, and he warns Whitebeard about him by name.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Shanks shows Whitebeard the scars and names the man who gave them (~ch. 434). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Teach takes a second fruit's power and lives. Marco's only explanation: his body is 'different' — said like a man quoting something he half remembers.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Yami-Gura double power shown at Marineford; Marco's remark (~ch. 577). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The flag has three skulls. The fandom has counted them for fifteen years.

theories.title: The twentieth family took the other side
theories.question: Twenty kingdoms won a war eight hundred years ago and their kings moved to the holy land. One royal family stayed home — and the world's rulers never forgave the desk where her name is written.

evidence.claim: The missing twentieth founder was Alabasta's queen, Lili — who declined the holy land and went home to her desert.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Nefertari founding-family thread laid out at the Reverie flashback (~ch. 1084). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Her letter home is signed Nefertari D. Lili. The family that stayed was a D. the whole time — royalty carrying the enemy's initial.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Cobra reads the signature; Imu is listening (~ch. 1085). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Why did one founder go home?
The signature settles the family; WHY she stayed is still the desert's secret.

theories.title: The pirate the world erased
theories.question: Before the Pirate King there was a crew so dangerous the government deleted the island they fell on. The men who survived that crew went on to rule the sea.

evidence.claim: Sengoku names him: Rocks D. Xebec, captain of a crew that held Whitebeard, Big Mom and Kaido at once, defeated at God Valley by Garp and Roger together — an island now missing from every map.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Sengoku's briefing on the Rocks Pirates (~ch. 957). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The reader is finally taken to God Valley — and the 'incident' turns out to have been a slave-hunt festival the Celestial Dragons threw on somebody's homeland.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Kuma's flashback reaches God Valley (~ch. 1096). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A deleted island, an erased captain, and a crew of future emperors.
The valley shown; Xebec's own face and fate still in the dark — and his flag flies again over a man named Teach.

theories.title: Somebody sits on the empty throne
theories.question: The world's proudest fiction is that no single ruler exists — twenty kings and an empty chair to prove it. The chair is not empty.

evidence.claim: Deep in Pangaea Castle, someone keeps a frozen straw hat the size of a giant. The reader is shown the vault before the owner.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The colossal straw hat in the freezer beneath Mary Geoise (~ch. 906). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The Five Elders — the top of the world — kneel. A figure named Imu climbs the empty throne like it was always theirs.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Imu revealed above the Gorosei at the Reverie (~ch. 908). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Imu acts on the page for the first time — and what happens to the room is not something a ruler does. It is something a weapon does.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Imu's power shown against Sabo and Cobra's chamber (~ch. 1085). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A vault, a hat, a chair. Existence confirmed at 908; everything else — name's meaning, age, nature — is abyss.

theories.title: The third weapon answers to the throne
theories.question: Poseidon is a princess. Pluton is a ship. The third ancient weapon has no face and no location — only a signature: islands stop existing.

evidence.claim: Lulusia Kingdom is removed from the world — not conquered, removed. Ordered from a throne room, delivered from the sky.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The destruction of Lulusia (~ch. 1060). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Vegapunk's broadcast ties the pieces: an energy source from the ancient past — the Mother Flame — powers the thing that erased Lulusia.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Vegapunk's message on the Mother Flame and the Lulusia strike (~ch. 1113). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Islands stop existing.
The fuel is named; the weapon itself — Uranus or not — has still never been called by name on the page.

theories.title: The emperor the elders will meet
theories.question: A pirate walks into the holy land, and the men who rule the world clear their schedule. Emperors do not get appointments. Family does.

evidence.claim: Shanks is received by the Five Elders themselves, to talk about 'a certain pirate'. No other emperor has ever been in that room as a guest.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Shanks' audience with the Gorosei (~ch. 907). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: The world meets Figarland Garling, champion of God Valley and supreme commander of the God's Knights — and the fandom meets a face it has seen before, under red hair.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Garling introduced at the Reverie aftermath (~ch. 1086); the resemblance to Shanks is fan-observed, not stated. NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Why does the holy land open its door to him?

theories.title: The revolutionary carries the weather
theories.question: Every time the most wanted man in the world stands on a page, the wind picks up. Nobody in the story has said his power out loud — which, at this depth of the iceberg, is evidence.

evidence.claim: At Loguetown, at the execution platform, at the exact moment his son needs it: a storm out of nowhere, and a stranger who smiles at the weather.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Loguetown gale as Buggy's blade falls (~ch. 100). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Dragon on a rooftop in Gray Terminal, and the wind arrives with him again — a pattern by now, not a coincidence.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Dragon at the Goa Kingdom fire in Ace/Luffy's flashback (~ch. 589). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The storm keeps showing up on time. The fruit remains unnamed.

theories.title: The world is going to drown
theories.question: A dead genius bought one broadcast with his life, and spent it on a weather report: the seas will rise, and the world as drawn on every map will go under.

evidence.claim: Vegapunk's message: the world will sink into the sea. Not might — will. The islands the reader has spent a thousand chapters visiting are on a clock.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The core claim of Vegapunk's posthumous broadcast (~ch. 1113). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: And it has happened before: an entire ancient world already sank once. The Grand Line's islands are the mountaintops of the last drowning.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Vegapunk on the sunken ancient world (~ch. 1114). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The claim is on the page; the flood is not. Whether the ending raises the sea — or the All Blue is what's left after — is the deepest live question on this chart.

theories.title: What is the One Piece?
theories.question: The title of the story. A man on an execution stand said it exists; a man on a battlefield spent his death repeating it; a crew that saw it laughed. Nobody has said what it is.

evidence.claim: Whitebeard's last words, chosen carefully with the whole world listening: the One Piece IS REAL. He detonates the age with a confirmation, not a clue.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Whitebeard's death at Marineford (~ch. 576). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Roger's crew finds it — and the panel shows the reader their backs and their laughter. Whatever it is, its first recorded property is that it is FUNNY.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The treasure seen off-page at Laugh Tale (~ch. 967). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Luffy tells the crew his dream's end — and the balloon is blank for the reader, the same trick the story pulled at Laugh Tale. The answer exists in-world and is being deliberately held off-page.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The hidden dream, told but not shown (~ch. 1060; first hidden at ~ch. 585). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: Confirmed to exist, never described. The bottom of the iceberg.

theories.title: What happened in the missing hundred years?
theories.question: History has a hole in it, eight hundred years back and a century wide, and reading about it is a capital crime. Governments do not execute archaeologists over nothing.

evidence.claim: Ohara: an island of scholars burned down to its library for reading the wrong stones. Professor Clover begins to name the kingdom that fell — and is shot mid-sentence.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Buster Call on Ohara; Clover's interrupted sentence (~ch. 395). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

evidence.claim: Vegapunk fills in a shape: an ancient kingdom of impossible technology, a war it lost to twenty allied kings, and a world that sank. The century wasn't erased because it was shameful — it was erased because it isn't over.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The broadcast's account of the ancient war (~ch. 1114). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: A hole where a century should be.
The war and the sinking are on the page now. The kingdom's name, its king, and what the fight was ABOUT are still down here in the dark.

theories.title: The ancient kingdom has a name
theories.question: Clover said the name of the fallen kingdom carried its whole meaning — that the name itself was the threat. He was killed one clause before saying it, and no one on the page has finished his sentence in over seven hundred chapters.

evidence.claim: 'That kingdom's name was—' is the exact point where the World Government opens fire. They did not shoot to stop a history lecture; they shot to stop a WORD.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Clover's death mid-sentence on Ohara (~ch. 395). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: One proper noun, withheld for the length of the entire story so far. When it lands, the iceberg flips over.

theories.title: The throne has had one occupant for eight hundred years
theories.question: Imu speaks of an eight-hundred-year-old queen like a personal grudge. Either the throne keeps very good diaries, or the person on it was in the room.

evidence.claim: Imu, of Lili: spoken in the first person, with the heat of someone remembering — not the distance of someone quoting the archive.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. Imu's words on Nefertari D. Lili during Cobra's audience (~ch. 1085). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga; the reading of tense and tone is interpretation, marked guess.

evidence.claim: The Five Elders manifest as something other than old men — the top of the government is not human in any charted sense, and their master sits above THEM.
evidence.source_ref: Hand-authored. The Gorosei's transformations at Egghead (~ch. 1110). NEEDS HUMAN VERIFICATION against the manga.

status_timeline.note: The perennial-youth reading — an immortality operation, or something older. Nothing on the page rules it out, which is exactly why it lives at the bottom.
